speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your first husband-- your wife's first husband is back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she wrote me to the army, when I was in the army, the Russian army. You already came through a number. You know, that she-- so she wrote me once. And I got a letter. She wrote me a letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't know, because later on, one letter disappeared, so I thought, maybe she is-- she decided to live with Schulem, you know, with her first husband. So I wrote a letter. And I didn't hear no more, because she wrote me right away, but she wrote that letter when he was there. Let me clarify something. She found her first husband while you were away with the Russian army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. When I was with the Russian army. Oh, sure. I was not there. I was in the Russian army. And he came like 1945, '45. And I was 'til 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the second letter, she wrote me right away. Listen, it's so-and-so. In the first letter, she didn't tell me nothing, that she didn't want to live with him or whatever. She told me, he's back and this and that, because she didn't want him to see. He was sitting when she was writing, you know. How did you feel when you got--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then she write right away a second letter. Leon, that's-- and that's the way it happened. He came. He wants to live with me. But my brother Mendle was with me. And I told him, my brother told him, forget about it. Forget about it. It never happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wrote me a letter that's [INAUDIBLE]. We'll go together. And I'm going to send him away to Poland. Let him go and let him find himself his luck. And that's what it is. So when we came to [INAUDIBLE]-- oh, yeah. That officer, well, he said is going to pick up our baggage and take us right on the train. So he told me, now, you have a uniform, a Russian uniform, a Secret Serviceman. Nobody will bother you. Soon you come on the train, nobody will check you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You come with me and with the baggage. And we put it right in the same wagon where you-- where you have on your-- on your passport. And when you come there, soon the train starts moving-- we had prepared a civilian uniform. Take out your military uniform, wrap it up in a bag, throw it out through the window, and change your clothes. You are complete difference person. You are not a soldier. You're never been a soldier. Nobody would know what's happened. He gave me the good idea, and that's the way we came to [INAUDIBLE] where we find her brother. And her brother-- her brother's first wife also got killed. He had two sons. They all got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he found himself a lady from another city, and he's supposed to get married. Now, to get married again, he's supposed to get married now by a real rabbi. When we married in the ghetto, there was a religious Jew. There was no more rabbis. So we really weren't--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was another couple what we were going together. They were not married yet either. So all three couples, you're supposed to get married. The meanwhile, you can't get married if you are not divorced. So she has to go to Krakow with her husband, with her first husband, and get a divorce. And rabbi brought a-- oh, a big table with all rabbis to tell you how the-- words in the Jewish is not so easy, why, what. So the rabbi said, in this-- in this-- whatever happened with you during the war, with Leon, it doesn't have nothing. [INAUDIBLE] easy you'll get your divorce. But anyway, to get a divorce, he wants to get Maurice. He wants to get his son. He wants us to get his son. But my wife say, we suffered so much. And I say, don't never agree on that to give him Maurice. So-- and they start getting arguments.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about Maurice?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] so I say--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about him, about the boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: About Maurice? I mean, like my own son. He was a little boy, everything. You know, he was with me in business all my life. He was going to college. And he was helping me in Paterson, New Jersey, and we had a restaurant there in Paterson, catering.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was always-- he came from college. He used to work. And later, he got married. He was a partner with me all my life. So going back to where you were getting married.

speaker: interviewee
text: So going back to this, so she went-- so we had a deal with him. I say, you know what, Schulem, you ask Maurice if he wants to go with you or he wants to go with Ruth. And he asked him, and Maurice say no. He start crying. He said never in life.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was already-- what? Maurice was already 10 or 11 years old. 10 years old. And then he say, no. And then he say, no, I'm going to take him anyway and this and that. So I think he said, he needs some money. He needs some money, because--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- in the true, what we find out later, that he had already had a girlfriend in-- when he came to Poland, you know. So he had a girlfriend. So he said, he wants money. So I gave him 8,000-- 8,000 zlotys, Polish zlotys. I had a chain and other things. I didn't have so much money, so I sold it to get money, buy an apartment 'til we move out from the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I gave him $8,000 and he went and they went through the-- the divorce. And two days later we all got married by a rabbi, three couples. We got married. So one couple went to Israel and his wife. And you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- I was waiting, got an uncle in the United States. I had two uncles and an aunt. He send me papers. But later on, when we were in the DP camp, my brother-- my brother-in-law also came to the United States, because he had an uncle. He sent them papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the only thing is-- the only mistake I made, I didn't put him right away on my-- on my name, Scher. I keep his name. I figure maybe he'll grow out, he'll be mad, so I left his name, Egert.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when they came to send papers, my uncle sent me Scher. But his name on the papers was Egert, Maurice Egert. So when it came already to the consul to go away from Bremen, they say, no, no. You can't go. He is Egert. He has to stay here. Whatever will happen, we don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I write to my uncle, send him a telegram to my uncle, to the United States, in Paterson. I need another-- another passport. And it was a lot of hard-- hard work. He was in the business, in the catering business. To tell-- he has to tell how much he make and all of this. He made that once. But later for another boy, it was not so easy, but he did it. And he said, it took three months. What was the name of this uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle, Sam Shapiro. I got him in the picture. Oh, what a good man. Wonderful man. He died just about five years ago. He was in catering. I also was partners later with him in catering for a while. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the DP camp where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: DP camp, Bad Reichenhall DP camp. That was the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there three years, 'til 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions--

speaker: interviewee
text: And later, 1948, we went to the-- to the city hall, to the German city hall, to marry, you know, in German, the city hall, to have, you know, married papers, you know, marriage papers in-- in Germany, marriage papers. 1949, Maurice was 13 years old. He had a bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the United--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camps, yeah. He had a little bar mitzvah, you know. And later, we came-- three months later, my brother-in-law left. We were supposed to go together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any more--

speaker: interviewee
text: In March.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any more children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, later Abie. After we married, you know, and Jewish law-- so lady Abie-- a year later Abie was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After 11 years. After 11 years. Maurice was 11 years older from Abie. Abie was born in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the DP camp. Yeah. Had plenty problems with Abie. He got sick. My wife had surgery, gallbladder surgery. And Abie was five weeks old. When he was born, he was 13 pounds. Big baby. It was in the papers, you know. But later when I gave him-- I couldn't take care of him. He was five months old. So I gave him to the hospital where my wife was. And they poisoned him with some kind of milk. He had a-- a stomach infection. And later, they didn't cure it, he had a brain infection. So this-- so this Abie, my son, he had a brain infection. In the middle of the night, he was already to die. So I begged them to give me a nurse and I went to Munich to a kinder clinic, a children clinic, specialize.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I brought them and the money, the professor, [INAUDIBLE], he came in and I told him all the story, what's happened with him. So he checked him out. And he say, 1 of 1,000 maybe can come out. Even when he come out from this, from the brain infection, he's going to have something missing in him or his eyes or not doing things, something will miss. So that's when he got on his eyes, so his eye, the eyeball wouldn't stay to look. His eyes keep going. He had problem in his college to make him also his tests.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good-- very good-- you know, very good mind. But to make, he has to read a lot. And couldn't read. He has to throw his whole head, not just throwing the eyes. Just throwing the whole head. He had problems. He had problems. He had already a girl involved helping him. And she was a Catholic. Our doctor's her daughter. She helped him with this tests, to come to the test for reading. And later he was involved and we didn't even know for three years. And later he say he wants to get married. He told his brother. So Maurice told us. And later, we went to our rabbi. We went to [INAUDIBLE] rabbi. We took him along.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is already-- this in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you leave-- when did you leave the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We leave the DP camp-- we leave July-- we went 11 days on the boat, so like July 1st we left Bremenhaven, 'Bremenhafen' in Bremen. We went through a boat-- with a boat. [INAUDIBLE], this was a military boat, an old military boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe to what city you arrive and the day you arrived to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we arrived in the United States, [INAUDIBLE] first we arrived, we didn't have where-- didn't have where to put the boat. There was not enough room. A lot of boats arrived that time, 1949, one on and off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said-- and my whole family came out to pick me up. My aunt, my uncles, cousins, they came out. But later, they let them know, because we can't-- the boat has to go back a couple of miles, three miles, and stay on the ocean. And next day-- they already it was next day boat should come in in New York. And then my cousins came out and my uncle the second day. What did you feel when you saw the Statue of Liberty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what I saw, it was a free world. A free, nice world. And then my family. I remember, my-- one of my uncles I remember, because he left 1923. So I remember this uncle. My aunt, she left 1906. My other uncle, the caterer, he left 1911 or '12.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you got to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I do?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for a living when you came to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what I did for a living. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My uncle opened up a restaurant for me for three months. First I was working in the shoe factory. But at the shoe factory, I don't like that. It was just a plain work and then was not enough work. I was making $40, $45. There was no work. I was making $27, $27.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my uncle say, you know what, you are a good cook to my wife. I'll open up a restaurant. He opened up a luncheonette and with refrigeration, a small luncheonette. And not in a famous place, but a nice place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were there, making out pretty good. But then one day close to a year, Gypsies moved in, and that whole strip-- a lot of Gypsies moved in. I don't know from where they came. And there was so much-- so much noise and so much problems. And they were going begging, but the customers, customers was afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I have to close that. I have to close, take all the fixtures to Coney Island.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was-- I was for three summers in Coney Island, had a beer garden. And then from there, after three summers I didn't like it. It was a gypsy life, living there, and only in the summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to work for a firm where they make stained glass windows, so I was doing the Jewish lettering. Before I came, they made the artists-- the main artist was a German. There was a lot of Polish and American. So when they made the Jewish lettering in the synagogues they make a little dot, like a [NON-ENGLISH] and a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, a [NON-ENGLISH], all mistakes. You came in to put the windows in the synagogue. The rabbi comes in, he looks, this whole window has to go out. This piece has to go out. You have to take all the windows out and change all the things they make. So when I came there, the boss gave me to work on the Jewish lettering. And I fired it also, in fire. Three times painting, three times fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked there for a while 'til we find a place and we bought a restaurant. We bought a restaurant in Paterson.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a big restaurant. Leon's. Started on my name. Leon's Restaurant. And I had it for four years. After that, I sold it and I went to-- to Plainfield, New Jersey, where I bought a big restaurant. New Yorkers bought my restaurant and I had nice profit and the restaurant, so I bought a big restaurant [INAUDIBLE], 200 seats. It was also-- in five years, there was six heads in five years. Big menus, but not much on the menu, and no prices. So they couldn't stay. I bought it for a very reasonable price. And we built it up. We built it up. We stayed 11 years, 'til came another-- another thing. We had riots. When the riots came to Plainfield, New Jersey, they killed a policeman. There were helicrafters. It was unbelievable what's going on. My restaurant was worth $120,000. And later, business fall down. People were afraid-- I have international, mostly Jewish-style restaurant. From a lot of cities they used to come to me to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your last job before you retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, I had to liquidate first the restaurants and sell it for $7,000 on auction. I had to sell it on auction. I have a 10 years lease, so I stayed another year. And after 11 years, I have to let the people go slowly. I let them go slowly, but later on I see I can't do it, pay the bills and people don't come and the rent was big and all the expenses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I sold it. I called in an auctioneer. Sold all the equipment, beautiful equipment, refrigerator, freezers, and all, for $7,000. And then went back with nothing. And what did you do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we bought a diner. We bought a diner for $45,000, $55,000. Then we build another-- only catering. 'Til now my son has the catering there. I left him with catering 15 years ago. But I usually go every year to help in the month of June with wedding, bar mitzvahs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many years have you been married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I've been married for 19-- really, married with-- from 1941, I figure. We lived together, you know, from 1941. It's '91. That's 40-- no, that's 41, '91, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90. That's 56 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: 56 Years. My wife got sick after we went the third time to Israel for two months. And then in Netanya we rented an apartment. We go all over Israel. We tried-- Israel was our-- our life and we never went no place else. We went to Israel. We love it. And then after we came back from Israel, about two, three months later, she started mixing in some words. Mix up my daughter-in-law, one daughter-in-law with the other daughter-in-law. Mixing in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on that time, it's about nine, 10 years when she got sick. And then she was really-- got Alzheimer disease. Suffered, take her to daycare centers. And later, after two-- two years ago I took her to a nursing home. When my sons came over, they said, Daddy, you can't go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to have help at the house. And I used to go out to play cards or whatever. I couldn't sit all the time. So I had somebody in the house taking care. Later I took her to the nursing home. And I went actually twice a day to the nursing home 'til she died, 1940-- 1995-- 1994, September, 1994. She passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now it's my children, grandchildren. I got 7-- How many grandchildren do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: 7 grandchildren. My oldest son has four, three boys, two lawyers, computer one-- one, and one girl. She is a master of psychology. So is my younger son, master of psychology. Now he's doing in aviation something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandchildren-- he got three children, one from his second wife. She is in college. And two kids, Amy and David. One is-- he is 15 and the girl is 17. Nice children, nice family. Used to visit every year almost, come to California and visit and go out and-- with the children. I have-- I have also five great-grandchildren, my older son.

speaker: interviewer
text: So have a nice family, that's for sure. And the two brothers live like really two brothers. They love each other. If one needs help, is there. Yeah. [INAUDIBLE] Thank God I'm healthy. How-- how did the Holocaust affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a while, you know, it affect very much. I missed-- I miss my-- my sisters and my brothers. I miss them. And my mother especially, she suffered without a father so many years. She never want to remarry, my mother. And the time when it start getting good, they moved to-- she moved to Krakow, then the war broke out. And that's it. But the war was a disaster for a lot of people. I saw a lot of things. Kids starving for food, typhus. I didn't even go-- they didn't let me in even the temple in Jaworow to see my cousin. She was 13 years old. I used to teach her also Hebrew. I was older from her. And I couldn't go and see her. They say, can't go in there. It's all typhus people. And that's the way she died there, from typhus. They used to taking out dead people every day, 10, 20, 30 dead people. That's the way they brought in also a lot of food. They took the dead people out on the cemetery. Over there was already speculators where they were bringing in all kind of stuff. And they-- they changed with all kinds of materials. And they brought in-- on the same wagons they brought in food. That's the way we managed-- with that food, we managed to have-- serve people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Holocaust affect the way or your belief in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, yes. In the beginning, yes. I said there is no god, there's no this. But later on, it changed. I belonged to an Orthodox synagogue in Paterson, New Jersey. We were in Paterson, New Jersey 11 years. And then when we came to Miami, I just went into a-- into a conservative synagogue, usually for my wife. She never learned too much Hebrew, no cheder, whatever, so she liked to go to-- to shul and sit together. In the Orthodox synagogue she was sitting separate and we were sitting separate. So I changed it. She was very happy. She was singing. She know all the Hebrew songs, [HEBREW]. Was a happy person, very happy, nice, good mother, good wife, good grandmother, great-grandmother. I remember her. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would you like to leave for your children and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, message? They should stay Jewish, number one, not to marry, but some of them did marry-- some of them did marry not Jewish. And keep going, you know, in the tradition. Keep the Jewish tradition. That's very important. Tradition for the Jews is very important. And keep raising the kids the same way, in the Jewish tradition. My son here, he does it. My son in New Jersey-- since I left-- I used to make the seders and everything. Our family-- and for strange people we had in our house for Pesach.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my middle grandson, the lawyer-- he is a corporation lawyer. But he learned mostly in cheder, in the Hebrew school. And he's very good, so he-- he make-- because my son doesn't know too much. But during the traveling and he was so young and he didn't go to cheder, so very little. So my grandson is the only one with this. And the other grandson also, my granddaughter know Hebrew. That's what they know. And that's it. Is there anything else you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything else? Our health. Our health. And I hope to go yet to Israel. This is my life. I never want to go no place else, to Israel. I love it. I love it. I like to visit Jerusalem, Haifa. We have my wife's cousin [INAUDIBLE] and Netanya. My cousins are still there. My cousins' children are there. They have farms. They raise flowers and also they have chicken. And [INAUDIBLE] the grand cousins, when I go to Israel, stay in a hotel, but visiting for the weekends they take me. They're good cooks, beautiful people, second cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Scher, would you please introduce me to your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my [NON-ENGLISH]. This is my [NON-ENGLISH], my lovely son. But I love him very much, so good nature. So good. He's got a lovely family. His name is Abe, Salomon Abe Scher. He's got a lovely family. And I love him. A lovely wife, [INAUDIBLE], blonde.

speaker: interviewer
text: Abe, what would you like to tell your father today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first thing is that I'm really, really proud of you for doing-- for having the courage to tell your story, to give this testimony, because it's-- I think it's important for my children and for Maurice's children and for their children and for other people to-- to hear what you went through, and the strength that it takes and the strength that you had to survive what you went through and what you-- what Mom went through, and-- and that if it wasn't for you and Mom, Maurice-- me and Maurice wouldn't be here and seven grandchildren and five great-grandchildren wouldn't be here, and probably a number of other people that you helped along the way, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: Also what I have to say, I didn't mention with Maurice, Maurice and his wife. He's got a lovely wife, a doll. We are always together. Never had a fight. We lived together for a while and we've been in business together. She is very lovely, very nice. And they have nice children. They are very happy. We are a very happy family, big, happy family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
